sentences.text: The front line, our front line was in the position, in the direction of Warsaw.
And we actually entered the outskirts of Warsaw after bitter fighting, I might say, bitter fighting.
Took us from July 23rd, from July 23rd to approximately middle of August when we enter Warsaw.
That was that outskirt.
That's the-- now, Praga Warszaqa, which is, is a town-- Praga Warszaqa is on the right bank of River Vistula.
The left bank is Warsaw.
To go to Warsaw, you have to cross a bridge, or you cross the waterway.
And you're right in Warsaw, in the center Warsaw.
We got there mid-- like middle of, of August.
Like the 19th or the 20 of August.
No, we did not have any orders to cross Warsaw until January 17th, 1945.
That's when the order came to cross Warsaw.
For all those months.
For all those months, we there.
If I-- in my opinion-- that's my opinion.
But in my opinion, if the front line would have progressed without stopping, without stopping--
In order-- in order, the way we came, we would have been able to take Warsaw.
That's my opinion.
General-- the generals came to a different conclusion.
They say, we lost too many people.
And our supplies lines are too far away.
Base like 300 kilometers away because the front progressed very rapidly from the Soviet Union to Poland.
And that was their explanation-- that they have to replenish their people with ammunition and people to replace the people, because-- yes, that's true.
We had awful lot of losses, no question about it.
We were not aware of-- yes, we were told about the uprising, not the uprising about the Jewish uprising.
Well, the Jewish uprising had been before that.
But while you were sitting there on the other side of the river--
We knew about the uprising.
--there was fighting going on in Warsaw.
Absolutely.
Yes, we heard it.
We heard it.
We heard the shooting.
But we did not know exactly, you know, how far the shooting is.
Are the Germans shooting, or the Polish resistance is shooting?
Because the Polish resistance started, as we know, the 1st of August.
And we were the middle of August.
We were already on the other side-- in other words, on the, on the right bank of Warsaw, on the west side of the-- of, of, of Warsaw.
So in mine opinion, if those two items would have been coordinating together, there's no question.
We would have.
I don't know we would have been able to occupy the whole Warsaw, because Warsaw is not a very tiny city.
But nevertheless, we would have been able to cross the river if we would have had cooperation from both sides, from across the river and from this side here.
One push, I'm pretty sure we would have been able to go over.
There were no bridges, but that's not a big thing, because pontoon can do it very nicely in military.
There was no fighting.
Everything was gone.
Everything was gone.
Now, while the position was quiet, while the position were quiet, there were-- there were-- there was some intention of doing so.
And as a matter of fact, one of my generals, the one-- at that time, he became already a general.
He was a colonel when he started to organize, when he took over for his division.
But at that time, he was already a general.
And he was-- he had in command two armies already, or three armies, as a matter of fact.
And he sent-- he sent-- he sent regimentals-- the third regiment from the third division he sent on his own.
That's how I know.
That's what we know, on his own.
If it was sent with coordination with the Soviet Union, I don't know.
But the fact is that he did send-- he did send the third regiment from the third division across the river, hoping to get across the river.
Unfortunately, as soon as they reached the banks, the river banks, the Germans open up tremendous fire.
And practically half of that regiment just had it.
And I lost my two friends.
One of them lost a leg.
His brother lost his life.
They were two brothers, and a couple other one, you know, injuries.
The Germans were fighting back, yes.
They were fighting very, very, very strongly back.
Yes, there was striking fiercely.
The city itself, Warsaw, no problem.
On the 17th of March, we came completely from a different direction.
Pontoons were already made.
Everything was created, you know, by the-- by the [INAUDIBLE].
And we had no problem enter Warsaw.
We took Warsaw without a shot.
Without a shot fired, we took Warsaw.
Believe-- that was in January 17.

From, from there, we progressed to the western part of, of Poland, to the western Pomerania, cities like Bydgoszcz, big cities, Torun, that area there, then a small town by the name of Chelmno, which, again, we were warned, there is a extermination camp there.
We were already--
We told-- before we saw it, yes.
Already we knew already about it.
What we saw there-- again, that was no big barracks, because they didn't have any barracks. there.
That was strictly extermination, one of the first one, which, as far as I remember, was initiated in December of 1941-- very, very primitive.
They started with this thing here until they develop those big camps like Auschwitz and the other ones.

And the way we, we were told is that there were-- people were brought there by buses, and that they were parked overnight in a church and stripped of their clothing and loaded into a special bus, which 50 or 75 people were able to get in there, and the gas was released in a short time, which took about 15 minutes.
It was something like 15 kilometers, something like that, to that forest.
And by the time that bus reached the station there, the people were dead.
The church absolutely was there.
The church was there.
Later, of course, I saw that the-- on pictures, on the video pictures here, that the church is still standing and they're still praying in that church.
That church is still there.
And what did I saw?
A lot of bones-- a lot of bones.
A lot of bones were there.
And of course, again, graves.
There were a lot of big graves.
Mass graves they had there.
And there were, again, a lot of ashes, a lot of ash.
And again, we were told that people were buried in that thing here.
They were no-- now, this camp was operating actually to the last-- to the last time for the occupation.
In other words, we, we entered-- as far as I know, we entered on the 25th or the 26th or something like in the 20s of January, that camp.
And that camp was still in production.
That camp was still in production.
Yes, Germans were there.
Germans were there.
We did not catch any, any living Germans there.
But Germans were there, because they were still there.
And there was also people from the area there, Poles who were living there.
But on the camp itself, we did not take any prisoners, because the, the next town from that point, that was our next crossing-- was our German city.
That was the first town, German town, which we took right from Chelmno, right on the same line.
And the town was Zlotow.
Zlotow was the first German city.
So they moved out fast.
They moved out fast.
But nevertheless, later, we found out, like everybody else, that two survivors were there, two young people.
And as far as I know, they're still alive in Israel-- but two boys.
They were found.
[INAUDIBLE].
Who had survived Chelmno.
Those are the only two people which, as far as I know-- which I found out later.
I did not see them there.
I did not see them there.
No
No, no, no, no.
We are in Germany.
Of course, the bitter fighting there going on.
And meanwhile, we are occupying one city after another.
And that's-- that cross which I show you, I won there in Pomerania.
Markisch Friedland was the town.
And the reason I did win that cross is because I was involved in destroying two German tanks, two target tanks.
I was-- my cannon was able to destroy those two, two tanks.
There were actually a big destruction there, because they tried to break through.
And altogether, they would destroy 25 of those tanks.
But my cannon shot it down.
A little bit farther down, just a little bit farther down, we captured-- we occupied a small village-- not a village, a farm-- pretty, pretty rich farm.
And they had all kinds of things there.
And it was the time close to, to Pesach, to Easter.
And you know, we-- there were lot of food there.
But we didn't have any eggs.
So we were looking for eggs.
And I went to a-- not a stable, a storage room where they had hay.
You know, straw and hay?
And I notice some-- something is moving on top of that hay.
The straw is moving, not the heads of the people.
So my first think was, who is there, you know, in German.
And somebody answered in German.
And the same word meant the same thing in Polish.
He answered, 'ja', me.
And-- but being with a group for so many years, you know every voice.
And it sounded suspicious.
And I said, you better hand-- in German, this time-- put your hands up and come out.
And that's why we got those 10 German soldiers.
Meanwhile, I called for reinforcement.
I mean, I was-- it was nothing to me, because I didn't fight them.
But nevertheless, I got those 10 Germans and one tank commander.
He lost his tank.
And there [INAUDIBLE] to that farm.
And, and they were waiting, actually.
He told me they had radios there, contact with the-- with the-- because it was close to the-- to the Baltic Sea.
And they had contact with the ships.
And they were waiting for a night to break out from there.
So you know, [INAUDIBLE] even said that we were very lucky that, that we caught those people, he said.
Because, you know, when you break out, you eventually you have to shoot at least one or two guards to break out.
But he was very brash.
And you know, I told him, listen.
He was a captain.
I said, I'm Jewish.
I spoke fluent German.
I still do.
But you know, in those days, it was even better.
I said, fluent German, I'm from Poland.
I'm a Jewish-- a Polish Jew.
But don't you be afraid that you're gonna be-- you're gonna meet the same fate what my people did in Poland.
I saw the camps.
I know what it is.
Oh, he said that he, he said he had nothing to do with destruction of the Jews.
He was just a tank commander.
And he was just a French soldier-- I mean, a front line soldier, and that was it.
So I said, don't worry.
You, you will not meet the same fate, what my people did.
You'll be well taken care of.
And that was the end of it.
We sent them away.
And what they did with them, I don't know.
But the fact is, they didn't kill them.
From there-- from there, a little bit farther.
We went to occupy the territory near Szczcin.
OK?
And about the 16th of-- not 16th-- by the 14th of, of April, we moved position.
We moved to the River Oder.
And on the 16th, we crossed River Oder under tremendous artillery barrage.
We crossed the River Oder.
And from there on-- there was very heavy fighting.
And, and then, from then on, our direction was to occupy city Berlin.
And slowly progressing, we got stuck for a couple of days in the Seelow Heights because there, there were bigger resistance there.
And by the way, the opposition of our-- on that front line was the SS Himmler Divisions.
So they were pretty good, pretty good.
And anyway, we occupied the, the, the Seelow Heights and went to the vicinity of Oranienburg.
And that's where we stumbled again on a concentration camp, this time concentration camp Sachsenhausen, which is in the city of Oranienburg.
We were told in advance that we will probably, probably get a concentration camp, probably, that we were not sure-- probably.
And it was taken well in advance.
And we caught a couple SS people there.
And we caught-- I mean liberated quite a few thousand of people.
And--
The first which I saw is, again, you know, barracks, again, buildings.
There were doors.
Always, you have to come through the--
Yeah, there were always wire.
Always were barbed wires.
You had to go through the gate.
You go through the gate.
We took the cannons in.
Infantry went first.

We went through the-- through the-- passed the infantry, following the infantry, went in, started to go to one, one barrack after-- people you didn't see immediately, because it was people-- was fighting, and they just sat in the barracks.
They were just hiding.
They didn't know the war was over, because there were still SS people in there.
So eventually, we entered one of the barracks.
And we entered in-- happens to be that that particular barrack has only ladies, or women, and from all Europe.
And I spoke with those four different language, five there, Yiddish including, five different languages.
So I started to throw names.
Everybody was crying, everybody hysterical, in terrible shape.
They were-- they were laborers there.
They were working in all kind of factories there.
And of course, malnutrition, because they were not fed-- and completely hysterical, completely hysterical.
You couldn't understand what they're saying.
And slowly and slowly, I was able to talk.
Now, again, we did not stay long there.
We did not stay.
None of those places we stayed more than an hour and a half to two hours.
But I was able to get what I wanted to and find out from where they-- mostly, they were from Hungary, from Poland, from Czechoslovakia.
We had people from all over, Jewish and not Jewish people.
A lot of Jewish people, but a lot of non-Jewish people also.
And finally, we went into couple of those factories.
There was one a complete tailor shop-- by the way, you know, my-- me, being a tailor what would you do?
You take a sewing machine.
I took it.
And I had to surrender that sewing machine in Berlin because they didn't let me take it home.
Can you imagine?
So in any event, complete, complete, complete equipped.
Nothing was destroyed.
You could go in and do any kind of work you were doing.
You could have done immediately, with fabrics and everything there.
And they had all kind of factories for airplane parts, all kind of of things for industry, all different buildings.
And altogether were many, many buildings and many, many people.
In my estimation, mine estimation, you know, at least 2,000 people which I saw, at least 2,000 people.
And of course, men in all different uniforms speaking all different languages.
There were British people which I caught, Russian people, which-- prisoner of wars, you know, something like that.
We had couple English people there-- all different-- all different languages, you had it.
And of course, Jews-- of course, Jews.
Two guards.
Just two for the whole--
But they were not in the front.
We just caught them-- two guards-- two.
And I must admit, our soldiers killed them.
They just saw too much to that point, and they didn't want to waste any time.
Before anybody said, don't do it, they were gone.
They dropped them in the sewers.
They dropped them in the sewers.
It's a shame.
But-- you know, a human being, but nevertheless, what you saw, what you saw to that point was just too much.
You just couldn't control the crowd.
So that-- those are the two enemy soldiers which we had.
Did you see in Sachsenhausen any piles of dead bodies?
We saw pile-- not only pile of dead bodies.
We saw pile of bodies still hanging on the hooks.
They had special erected places for-- as a shooting gallery, and the bodies there, blood splattered all over the place, and bodies of-- starvation bodies.
And there's strictly bodies which they hung up.
And they must have done that, you know, in the last couple hours.
They must have done that in last couple hours, because the blood was still fresh blood and everything else.
As I say, Sachsenhausen was taken in advance of time.
And they probably had prepared a way of getting out.
The town of Oranienburg was 25,000 people.
I interrogated the people on the outside.
I did interrogate the people on the outside.
And of course, it's an old story.
The Germans simply did not admit-- they didn't know.
They didn't know what was going on.
How can you not know?
You have the railroad tracks, the, the trucks bringing in people.
The town consists of 25,000, which is very little.
People by the thousands are coming in.
Nobody's going out.
How can you not know what is being done there?
Yet they would not admit.
They wouldn't admit.
Under no condition they said that was-- people were dying there.
We knew that people were working in there, but not that people were dying in there.
People were dying.
Whoever was dying or whoever felt like-- whoever they felt is going to die, put them in the crematorium, because they had crematoriums.
They had crematoriums.
And they replaced them with people which still were able to work, simple as that.
And they wouldn't admit.
They wouldn't admit that they, they didn't-- they didn't know what was going on.
Don't tell me.
The Germans are not stupid.
They knew what was going on.
Whereas the Poles who lived around the camps, they would say--
They said it openly.
They said it openly.
And the Russians would say about the killings.
Exactly.
But the Germans didn't know.
But the Germans would not say nothing.
And if, if somebody you had already caught, interrogated, like a soldier or something, he had orders to do it.
That was the excuse.
You can do it.
Same thing.
Same thing.
In Oranienburg, not longer than two hours.
Left, left in, in, in front positions-- in other words, in advancing positions.
So that-- the Oranienburg was about, about the 20th-- about the 20th, or maybe the 21st.
I don't remember, really.
Of April-- OK?
About the 20th or the 21st of April-- and the direction was there were small towns-- to burn down everything.
You have to occupy that.
You had to cross another river to go through Berlin, the River Spree.
And finally, we crossed the River Spree and encircled the whole Berlin.
Once we crossed the river, we encircled the whole Berlin.
And from then on was hand-to-hand fighting, really.
I was 12 days in Berlin, including the Reichstag.
My cannon was firing, the first cannon put the first salvo from my battery was that Jewish boy on Reichstag.
We were firing on Reichstag.
And we did not go to the Reichstag, because I was artillery man.
We were simply pursuing street by street.
And finally, we ended on the 2nd of May.
And we-- the irony to the whole thing is-- I didn't know, of course, then-- we ended on Alexanderplatz.
Alexanderplatz was really the centrum of the Jewish community there in Berlin.
I met a couple of people here which-- were lucky to get out in 1936.
And they were living-- [?
Kuhn ?] is his name, Seymour [?
Kuhn. ?] He lives in Pomona.
And he used to live in Alexanderplatz.
Then I met couple of businesspeople in New York City.
They lived in Alexanderplatz.
Cannon.
Cannon, block by block.
Block by block.
But you didn't have to get off and--
No.
Absolutely not.
Not.
Now, the thing is it's also a lucky shot, because there was a cannon standing next to us.
Now, we occupied that particular spot, where we occupied, our last position was just a day before finish.
OK?
Day before finish we occupy that position.
And there were five other items which were destroyed on that position.
There were-- there was couple tanks, and there were four cannons destroyed on that same position.
Mine was lucky.
We didn't.

A friend of ours from the next battery, from the-- from next battery was standing-- you know, you don't place the cannons next to each other-- about 50 feet, 50, depends on the-- on the radius-- sometimes 50, sometimes 70 feet apart, 75 feet apart.
They were standing next.
They had a direct hit and, their whole crew just got wiped out.
So I was just lucky.
I got two shrapnels, that's true-- did not entered my uniform.
When we were-- when we had that long stay in-- near the city of Warsaw there in Prag, I made myself a uniform.
[LAUGHS] From-- I-- from-- I couldn't wear a coat.
I was just too bulky.
So I took the fabric from the coat and made a uniform out of it-- you know, jacket.
And I made one for my captain.
And because I didn't wear any coats, I had to pad it out should we stay warm.
So that padding saved my skin anyway.
I don't know if that would have killed me or not-- but saved my skin-- just two shrapnels embedded in that cotton, didn't go through.
So-- but that was the, the one day two of my guys got hit, minor injuries, from shrapnels.
So then the next day, they surrendered?
The next day, on, on the 2nd of May-- on the 2nd of May at 2:00 AM, they surrendered.
Crying a lot-- crying a lot.
Nothing.
Hug each other, and that was the end.
That was the end.
We didn't do anything.
There was no shooting anymore going on.
That center were completely cleared from Germans.
Eventually, all Germans surrendered anyway on the 8th.
But Berlin as such surrendered officially on that day.
And no shooting was going.
The only thing what we observed after that-- there were columns, thousands and thousands of German soldiers.
They didn't tell them where to go.
There was just signs posted-- this and this division or this and this regiment, go to here and there on this strasse.
And they were just following.
And thousands of them were passing by.
And what we saw later is, of course, you know, cleaning up the bodies and everything else.
We have to do it.
There were a lot of bodies there in Berlin.
In Berlin, we stayed till the 9th, which is approximately a week after the end.
On the 9th of May, the orders came to move to Poland.
And we moved to Poland.
Took us 10 days.
They wanted to go slow to get washed and everything else, you know, slowly, to get mentally accepted.
And we moved to Siedlce.
Our division originally supposed to have moved to Warsaw, but because there was no Warsaw, there was no buildings there.
In Siedlce, there was a division standing there before the war.
And we occupied those, those buildings.
Those buildings were standing.
And we stayed in Siedlce.
And like, in between, there was a lot of things going on, because the partisans were still fighting.
They were fighting the Jews, believe it or not, and they were fighting the, the, the other groups there.
They were shooting on the soldiers.
And we had to clear the forest at some-- what?
Did he came in?
Yeah.
OK.
That-- so we had to shoot the, you know-- clear the forest from them.
And we were called in after there were-- six Jewish people were killed in Lublin, believe it or not.
And that was already in June after we got back.
That was towards the end of June.
Six people were killed.
And there was a big struggle going on to clearing the forest.
And we took in over 2,000 of those AK people which didn't want to give up.
They still only--
Yes.
Capture and detain them.
And detain them.
Right.
Yes.
Detain them.
There were-- some of them were prosecuted, you know, [INAUDIBLE] thing.
Eventually, they were released.
Nobody got shot except in hand-to-hand fighting.
But whoever they thought did wrong got the lessons.
And whatever they did with them-- five years or two years or whatever-- I had nothing to do with it.
But we had to clear the forest.
And that was-- so that was the role of the army in Poland--
That was the--
That's after the war.
They wouldn't do anything if they wouldn't bother anybody.
But they shot people, unfortunately, people which came from the concentration camp.
And one of the people which they shot there was a rabbi's son, which was very, very involved in the uprising, I found out later-- in the uprising of Sobibor in 1942.
Yeah, and he was killed.
There was [INAUDIBLE]-- he was killed.
He was killed.
He just-- one of them got just married.
There were five, five men and one girl.
They just got married.
They were all wiped out, just like that.
Not too much, not too much.
I think about not more than six weeks.
I was involved with one of those big raids.
And that was the end for me.
That was the end for me, because I put in for the-- for the retirement from my army duty.
And eventually--
Yes, that was the point-- that's right.
Otherwise, I would have to stay for another three years.
And I know my friends did it, for-- stayed for another two years.
Good.
